items.question_text: Use FIG 5-8-1. Two successive departures use the same runway, radar identification will be established within 1 mile of the runway end, and their courses diverge by at least 15 degrees immediately after departure. What radar departure minimum applies?
items.explanation: FIG 5-8-1 supports the 1-mile minimum for successive departures from the same runway when courses diverge by at least 15 degrees immediately after departure and wake turbulence separation is not required.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-8-2 and FIG 5-8-3. Parallel departure runways are separated by less than 2,500 feet. Which condition supports the 1-mile same-airport departure minimum?
items.explanation: FIG 5-8-2 and FIG 5-8-3 illustrate parallel-runway/takeoff-course cases under the successive-departure rule. Courses must diverge by at least 15 degrees immediately after departure.

choices.text: Courses diverge by at least 15 degrees immediately after departure.
Courses remain parallel until both aircraft are radar identified.
Runways are separated by at least 700 feet regardless of course.
The second departure is assigned the same SID.

items.question_text: Use TBL 5-8-1 and FIG 5-8-4. RNAV SID departures from parallel runways diverge no later than 8 NM from the farther DER. What centerline separation and release stagger pair applies?
items.explanation: TBL 5-8-1 lists the no-later-than-8-NM divergence row as 1,130 feet minimum centerline separation with a 3,000-foot release distance stagger.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-8-5 and FIG 5-8-6. Simultaneous departures are in the same direction from parallel runways/takeoff courses separated by at least 2,500 feet. What course divergence is required immediately after departure?
items.explanation: FIG 5-8-5 and FIG 5-8-6 support simultaneous same-direction departures from sufficiently separated parallel surfaces when courses diverge by at least 15 degrees immediately after departure.

choices.text: No divergence is required after radar identification.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-8-7 through FIG 5-8-9. For simultaneous departures from nonintersecting or intersecting runway/helipad courses, what common premise must be true before applying the depicted radar departure minima?
items.explanation: FIG 5-8-7, FIG 5-8-8, and FIG 5-8-9 are still radar-departure applications: radar identification must be established within 1 mile and the required course divergence must exist.

choices.text: Radar identification will be established within 1 mile and required divergence exists.
The aircraft are both VFR and visual separation is in use.
The runways have the same magnetic heading.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-8-10 and FIG 5-8-11. A departure runway and an arrival runway have even parallel thresholds. What runway centerline spacing supports simultaneous departure/arrival operations if the departure course diverges at least 30 degrees from the missed approach course?
items.explanation: FIG 5-8-10 and FIG 5-8-11 illustrate the even-threshold case: runway centerlines must be at least 2,500 feet apart, with the required 30-degree departure-course divergence from the missed approach course.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-8-12 and FIG 5-8-13. The arriving aircraft is approaching the nearer runway, centerlines are less than 2,500 feet apart but at least 1,000 feet apart. What stagger relationship is required?
items.explanation: FIG 5-8-12 and FIG 5-8-13 support the nearer-runway stagger rule: landing thresholds must be staggered at least 500 feet for each 100 feet less than 2,500 feet of centerline separation.

choices.text: 500 feet of threshold stagger for each 100 feet less than 2,500 feet of centerline separation.
100 feet of threshold stagger for each 500 feet less than 2,500 feet of centerline separation.
A fixed 2,500-foot threshold stagger, regardless of centerline separation.
No stagger is required if the aircraft are radar identified.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-8-14. The arriving aircraft is approaching the farther runway in a staggered-threshold parallel runway setup. What relationship must the runway centerline separation satisfy?
items.explanation: FIG 5-8-14 supports the farther-runway rule: runway centerline separation must exceed 2,500 feet by at least 100 feet for each 500 feet the landing thresholds are staggered.

choices.text: It must exceed 2,500 feet by at least 100 feet for each 500 feet of threshold stagger.
It may be less than 2,500 feet if the farther threshold is staggered.
It must be exactly 2,500 feet with no threshold-stagger adjustment.
It must exceed 8,300 feet whenever thresholds are staggered.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-8-15. Nonintersecting runways are used for simultaneous departure and arrival operations. Which runway-geometry condition supports the operation?
items.explanation: FIG 5-8-15 illustrates the nonintersecting diverging runway case: runways diverge by 15 degrees or more and runway edges do not touch.

choices.text: Runways diverge by 15 degrees or more and runway edges do not touch.
Runways diverge by less than 15 degrees but share a departure end.
Runway centerlines are at least 700 feet apart, with no divergence requirement.
The arrival runway is always farther from the tower.

items.question_text: Use TBL 5-9-1. An aircraft will intercept final less than 2 miles from the approach gate. What is the maximum interception angle?
items.explanation: TBL 5-9-1 limits final approach course interception to 20 degrees when the interception point is less than 2 miles from the approach gate, or when triple simultaneous approaches are in use.

items.question_text: Use TBL 5-9-1. A fixed-wing aircraft will intercept final 2 miles or more from the approach gate. What maximum interception angle applies?
items.explanation: TBL 5-9-1 permits 30 degrees at 2 miles or more from the approach gate for fixed-wing aircraft. The 45-degree value is the helicopter exception.

items.question_text: Use TBL 5-9-1. A helicopter will intercept final 2 miles or more from the approach gate. What maximum interception angle is allowed?
items.explanation: TBL 5-9-1 allows 45 degrees for helicopters when the interception point is 2 miles or more from the approach gate.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-9-1. An aircraft is established on a published segment of the instrument approach procedure before the approach gate. What key item may ATC issue after giving required arrival information?
items.explanation: FIG 5-9-1 supports issuing approach clearance once the aircraft is established on a segment of a published route or instrument approach procedure, after the required arrival instructions are provided.

choices.text: A landing clearance from approach control.
A clearance to descend below all published altitudes.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-9-2. An aircraft is being vectored to an intermediate fix for an RNAV approach and is not yet established. What altitude instruction must be included with the clearance?
items.explanation: FIG 5-9-2 supports assigning an altitude to maintain until the aircraft is established on a published route or instrument approach procedure segment.

choices.text: Maintain an assigned altitude until established on the published segment.
Maintain VFR-on-top until the final approach fix.
No altitude is required if direct to an intermediate fix.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-9-3. Before the aircraft reaches the approach gate, which package of instructions is the core requirement for a radar arrival that is not a radar approach?
items.explanation: FIG 5-9-3 belongs to the arrival-instructions rule: position relative to a final approach fix/NAVAID/airport, vector to intercept if required, approach clearance when eligible, and communication instructions.

choices.text: Position relative to final, vector if required, approach clearance when eligible, and communication instructions.
Only the final approach course heading and a frequency change.
Only landing clearance and runway exit instructions.
Traffic advisories only, because the pilot manages the approach gate.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-9-4. Simultaneous dependent approaches are being conducted with adjacent runway centerlines at least 2,500 feet but no more than 3,600 feet apart. What diagonal radar separation is required between successive aircraft on adjacent final approach courses?
items.explanation: FIG 5-9-4 supports the 1-mile diagonal radar separation standard for simultaneous dependent approaches when runway centerlines are at least 2,500 feet but no more than 3,600 feet apart.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-9-5. Runway centerlines are more than 3,600 feet but no more than 8,300 feet apart. What diagonal radar separation applies between successive aircraft on adjacent final approach courses?
items.explanation: FIG 5-9-5 supports 1.5 miles diagonal radar separation for runway centerlines more than 3,600 feet but no more than 8,300 feet apart.

items.question_text: Use FIG 5-9-6. Runway centerlines are more than 8,300 feet but no more than 9,000 feet apart. What diagonal radar separation applies between successive aircraft on adjacent final approach courses?
items.explanation: FIG 5-9-6 supports 2 miles diagonal radar separation for runway centerlines more than 8,300 feet but no more than 9,000 feet apart.
